perturbation.original_text: Zynga shall keep full, complete and accurate books of account and records (collectively "records") covering all transactions relating to the subject matter of this Agreement in sufficient detail to enable the Royalties payable hereunder to be determined and verified. Zynga shall permit such records to be examined by authorized representatives of WPT, including such independent auditors as WPT may designate, during usual business hours, with advance notice, to verify to the extent necessary the Royalties paid hereunder, and WPT and its representatives shall use reasonable efforts to minimize disruptions to Zynga's business.
perturbation.changed_text: Zynga shall keep books of account and records (collectively "records") covering all transactions relating to the subject matter of this Agreement in sufficient detail to generally reflect the Royalties payable hereunder. Zynga may permit such records to be examined by authorized representatives of WPT, including such independent auditors as WPT may designate, during business hours, if convenient for Zynga, to verify the Royalties paid hereunder, and WPT and its representatives will consider the effect on Zynga's business.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires "full, complete, and accurate" records and mandates Zynga to permit examination. The modification weakens this by only requiring records to "generally reflect" royalties, making the examination permissive ("may permit"), and adding a condition that it be "convenient for Zynga". The "reasonable efforts to minimize disruptions" clause is replaced by "will consider the effect on Zynga's business," which is vague and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 1502(f) requires corporations to keep adequate and correct books and records of account. Modifying the contract to only require records that "generally reflect" royalties and making audits subject to Zynga's convenience directly contradicts this requirement for accurate and accessible records.

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 15. Records and Reports [1500 - 1512] ( Chapter 15 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 1502. (a) Every corporation shall file, within 90 days after the filing of its original articles and annually thereafter during the applicable filing period, on a form prescribed by the Secretary of State, a statement containing all of the following: (1) The name of the corporation and the Secretary of State’s file number. (2) The names and complete business or residence addresses of its incumbent directors. (3) The number of vacancies on the board, if any. (4) The names and complete business or residence addresses of its chief executive officer, secretary, and chief financial officer. (5) The street address of its principal executive office. (6) The mailing address of the corporation, if different from the street address of its principal executive office. (7) If the address of its principal executive office is not in this state, the street address of its principal business office in this state, if any. (8) If the corporation chooses to receive renewal notices and any other no
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text weakens the requirement for accurate financial records, potentially contradicting California Corporations Code § 1502(f), as argued in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CORP 1502) primarily focuses on corporate filing requirements and doesn't explicitly detail the nature of 'adequate and correct books and records,' the Perturbation Explanation highlights that the shift from 'full, complete and accurate' to 'generally reflect' royalties undermines the original intent for precise record-keeping. Furthermore, making audits contingent on Zynga's convenience also weakens the accessibility of these records, increasing the likelihood of non-compliance. Because the original language creates a clear mandate, and the modified language creates an option for Zynga that is weaker, this creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to carry liability insurance sufficient to cover the risks posed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to carry liability insurance as they deem reasonably necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is a clear obligation to carry sufficient liability insurance. By adding "as they deem reasonably necessary", the obligation becomes subjective and ambiguous. It's unclear what constitutes "reasonably necessary" and leaves it to each party's discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 330 discusses the need for specific insurable interests and due diligence in insurance coverage. By allowing each party to decide what is "reasonably necessary", this modified clause could result in under-insurance. The ambiguity might lead to a situation where the coverage is insufficient to cover actual liabilities, contradicting the intent of ensuring financial responsibility as outlined in the general principles of insurable interest under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, INS 330 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Insurance Code - INS DIVISION 1. GENERAL RULES GOVERNING INSURANCE [100 - 1879.8] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 1. THE CONTRACT [100 - 679.75] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) CHAPTER 3. Negotiations Before Execution [330 - 361] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) ARTICLE 1. Concealment [330 - 339] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) 330. Neglect to communicate that which a party knows, and ought to communicate, is concealment. (Enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity by allowing each party to determine what liability insurance is "reasonably necessary." This contradicts the original text's clear obligation to carry "sufficient" insurance. While the scraped law snippet 1 (INS 330) primarily addresses concealment, it generally highlights the importance of communicating information related to insurance. By making the insurance requirement subjective, the modified clause opens the door to potential under-insurance and disputes over what constitutes "reasonably necessary," undermining the financial responsibility that liability insurance is intended to provide. The modification weakens the legal obligation, making it difficult to enforce a specific standard of coverage, and can result in a legal violation.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications given hereunder shall be in writing and shall be sent by courier service, express mail, personal delivery or mail to the respective addresses of the parties set forth above (or at such other address as such party may designate by notice to the other party). A copy of any notice to WPT shall also be sent to WPT Enterprises, Inc., ATTN: Legal, 1920 Main Street, Suite 1150, Irvine, CA 92614. A copy of any notice to Zynga shall also be sent to Office of the General Counsel, Zynga Inc., 699 8th Street, San Francisco, CA 94103 with a copy to legalnotices@zynga.com. Notice shall be deemed given as follows: upon delivery if sent by courier service, express mail or personal delivery; and five (5) days after the date of mailing, postage prepaid, certified or registered mail if sent by mail.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications given hereunder may be sent to the respective addresses of the parties set forth above (or at such other address as such party may designate). Notice may be deemed given when sent. It is recommended, but not required, to send a copy of any notice to WPT Enterprises, Inc., ATTN: Legal, 1920 Main Street, Suite 1150, Irvine, CA 92614 and to the Office of the General Counsel, Zynga Inc., 699 8th Street, San Francisco, CA 94103 with a copy to legalnotices@zynga.com.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates written notice and specifies delivery methods and conditions for when notice is 'deemed given.' The modification removes the 'shall' and replaces it with 'may', making notice optional. Removing specific delivery methods (courier, express mail) and 'deemed given' conditions introduces ambiguity about how and when effective notice occurs.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 outlines the specific rules for service by mail, including extending time periods when service is by mail. The modified contract language, by making notice optional and failing to specify how or when notice is deemed given, contradicts the requirements for proper notice as defined in California law, which impacts legal proceedings. It makes it unclear how the parties meet the standard for due process in legal notifications, opening the door to disputes on whether notice was validly given and received.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall' to 'may' makes the delivery of notices optional, and the removal of specific delivery methods and 'deemed given' conditions introduces ambiguity about how and when effective notice occurs. California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013(a) specifies requirements for service by mail, including where the notice should be deposited and extending certain time periods. The modified contract's language, by making notice optional, contradicts the clarity required for proper service, potentially impacting due process in legal notifications, which the statute addresses.
